title: Mid-2026 M&A Insights: AI Drives a Recovery, but Questions Remain
gist: Global M&A rebounded ~28% in H1 2026 to ~$1.6 trillion driven by AI-related strategic necessity, but the recovery is narrow and cautious: megadeals surged while smaller deal volumes stayed subdued, and technology posted the lowest deal sentiment even as it led deal value. The market bifurcates between richly valued AI infrastructure and correcting application-layer/traditional software assets, with financial services, energy, and health care carrying durable momentum.

insights.claim: Global M&A deal value reached approximately $1.6 trillion in H1 2026, up about 28% year over year and the strongest first-half total since the 2021-2022 boom, though slightly below H2 2025.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Headline value growth signals a real reopening of the deal market, but the sequential dip from H2 2025 warns against extrapolating a straight-line boom.
insights.quote: Global deal value reached approximately $1.6 trillion, up 28% from the same period in 2025 and slightly lower than in the second half of 2025.

insights.claim: The recovery is concentrated in the largest transactions while broader deal volumes, especially for smaller deals, remain subdued, making it a normalization rather than a return to boom conditions.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Investors should read strength as narrow and selective; breadth (mid-market volume) is the tell for whether the recovery is genuinely broadening.
insights.locator: The recovery is real, but it remains a normalization

insights.claim: There were 31 megadeals of $10 billion or more in H1 2026, up from 17 a year earlier and the highest first-half total since the 2021 boom, while activity in deals above $500 million softened to its long-term average.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: The megadeal surge alongside softer large-deal activity signals stronger valuation discipline and a barbell market concentrated at the very top.
insights.quote: There were 31 megadeals (transactions valued at $10 billion or more) in the first half of 2026—up from 17 during the same period last year

insights.claim: Large deals continue to be carried out predominantly in North America or by North American acquirers.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Geographic concentration means the deal recovery's durability is tied to North American corporate and sponsor appetite.
insights.locator: Large deals continue to be carried out

insights.claim: The market is driven less by broad optimism than by strategic necessity, with companies transacting where competitive pressure is greatest and remaining disciplined elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Necessity-driven dealmaking favors sectors facing existential AI or structural disruption over opportunistic bargain-hunting.
insights.quote: The result is a market driven less by broad optimism than by strategic necessity.
insights.locator: The result is a market driven

insights.claim: BCG's M&A Sentiment Index rose to 84 in Q2 2026 from 79 entering the year but remains below its long-term average of 100, combining fundamental drivers with GenAI-based analysis of corporate communications.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: A sub-100 reading confirms the recovery has room to run but is not yet at full confidence, tempering bullish positioning.

insights.claim: Europe holds the highest regional sentiment reading at 101 and is the only major region above the long-term average, but its momentum is softening, while the Americas sit at 84 and Asia-Pacific has rebounded to 55 from a recent all-time low.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Europe's leadership may be peaking; watch for a rotation as its uptick cools and Asia-Pacific recovers off a low base.
insights.locator: The more revealing story is the divergence

insights.claim: Japanese companies have become increasingly active global dealmakers, reflecting corporate governance reform and growing investor pressure to deploy underutilized capital.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Japanese outbound M&A is a structural, reform-driven source of demand worth tracking for cross-border targets.

insights.claim: At the sector level, financial services leads deal sentiment at 108 while technology has fallen to the lowest reading at 52 despite generating the most first-half deal value.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: The activity-versus-confidence gap in tech is the market's defining paradox and a signal to demand valuation discipline in AI deals.
insights.quote: financial services leads the field at 108, comfortably above the overall long-term average, while technology has fallen to the lowest reading at 52

insights.claim: Corporate and private equity hold nearly $2 trillion of undeployed capital and financing conditions are workable despite rates above post-2020 lows, building pressure to transact via both acquisitions and divestitures.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: A ~$2 trillion dry-powder overhang plus LP distribution pressure creates a structural tailwind that can convert to deal flow when exit windows open.
insights.locator: Corporate and private equity still hold

insights.claim: Geopolitical risk remains the defining constraint in 2026, with energy price volatility, trade friction, and conflict-related uncertainty complicating deal underwriting and extending timelines in cross-border and energy-intensive transactions.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Deal timelines and underwriting risk are elevated in industrial and cross-border deals; a hawkish rate surprise could test the market's macro sensitivity.

insights.claim: The AI value chain bifurcates: infrastructure-layer assets like data centers, computing, and power command extraordinary valuations on contracted demand, while application-layer company valuations are correcting.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: The correction opens more attractive entry points in the application layer for H2 2026, but only for buyers who can tell differentiated capability from AI-enthusiasm beneficiaries.
insights.quote: The defining dynamic is bifurcation: infrastructure-layer assets—data centers, computing, and power—command extraordinary valuations on the basis of visible demand under contract, while the valuations of application-layer companies are correcting.

insights.claim: Software-as-a-service business models face the greatest structural pressure because their exposure to AI disruption is highest.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Traditional SaaS is a disruption-risk zone; investors should discount AI-exposed software rather than assume recurring-revenue durability.
insights.locator: The unease is especially strong in business models

insights.claim: A wave of large-scale technology IPOs including SpaceX (already listed) with OpenAI and Anthropic expected to follow could reset risk appetite by validating private valuations and reopening a primary exit channel for sponsors.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Success or failure of these marquee AI IPOs is the single most important catalyst to watch for whether the deal recovery broadens in H2 2026.
insights.quote: SpaceX has recently listed, with OpenAI and Anthropic expected to follow.
insights.locator: One catalyst may come from the public markets

insights.claim: Cybersecurity continues to attract strong M&A interest as AI-enabled threats expand the attack surface and regulatory requirements become more demanding.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Cybersecurity is a durable, demand-driven M&A theme insulated from the AI valuation debate.

insights.claim: Defense and digital infrastructure have emerged as a distinct investment theme supported by national security spending, subject to industrial policy, sovereignty concerns, and government priorities that create their own valuation and regulatory drivers.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Sovereignty-linked assets carry government-driven demand but heightened regulatory and diligence complexity, changing how to underwrite them.

insights.claim: Financial services is the only sector where strategic imperatives clearly outweigh macroeconomic caution, with the highest sentiment at 108 as incumbents acquire scale, capabilities, and technology they cannot build fast enough.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Financial services is the highest-conviction sector for H2 2026; principal constraint is regulatory and political friction, not buyer appetite.
insights.quote: Financial services is the only sector where strategic imperatives clearly outweigh macroeconomic caution.

insights.claim: European banking consolidation is increasingly compelling as scale disadvantages, margin pressure, rising AI investment needs, and a more permissive regulatory environment erode the economics of remaining independent, though deals face lengthy politically sensitive approvals.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: The bottleneck for European bank M&A is execution and political approval, not rationale, so approval processes are the key catalyst or blocker to watch.
insights.locator: In European banking, the case for consolidation

insights.claim: Health care sentiment improved to 92 driven by large pharma facing a looming wave of late-decade patent expirations, turning to bolt-on M&A of biotech with validated platforms rather than transformational mergers.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Patent-cliff-driven bolt-on demand and GLP-1 competition make differentiated biotech platforms premium targets; contingent structures bridge valuation gaps.

insights.claim: In energy and utilities (sentiment 90), power availability has become the binding constraint on AI-driven growth, with hyperscalers moving from contracting for power to owning generating capacity and nuclear including small modular reactors moving to the center.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: The power-demand thesis pulls capital across the entire power value chain; nuclear and power infrastructure are the fastest-moving subthemes to position in.
insights.quote: power availability is now the binding constraint on AI-driven growth, and securing it is reshaping the sector's M&A.

insights.claim: Automotive is the outlier facing structural rather than cyclical challenges including overcapacity, EV-transition capital demands, and the shift to software-defined vehicles, making necessity not opportunity the predominant deal driver.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Automotive dealmaking is defensive restructuring and supplier consolidation, not growth; expect JVs, targeted software buys, and distressed consolidation.

insights.claim: The consumer sector (sentiment 71) is bifurcating, with pressured mass-market brands generating carve-outs and divestitures while premium, health-and-wellness, and DTC brands attract intense competition, and GLP-1 adoption prompts portfolio repositioning across food and beverages.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: The consumer play is a barbell: buy scarce premium growth platforms while sellers divest weak mass-market assets; GLP-1 is reshaping food and beverage portfolios.

insights.claim: In industrials, defense and aerospace remain the highest-conviction area on structurally rising defense spending and multiyear procurement, while traditional manufacturing stays subdued with activity concentrated in carve-outs and portfolio rationalization.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Within industrials, favor defense, automation, and electrical/power equipment over traditional manufacturing when allocating deal capital.

themes.theme: Private Equity, VC and Exit Risk

insights.claim: Private equity has reemerged as a key driver of 2026 deal activity, flush with nearly $2 trillion of undeployed capital and compelled by LP distribution pressure, deploying via add-ons, take-privates, carve-outs, and continuation vehicles.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: A sustained IPO reopening would clear the exit backlog and act as an additional catalyst for sponsor-driven deployment.

insights.claim: Risks are building in parts of the private credit market and around software-heavy portfolios, while an increasing number of buyouts done at peak 2021-2022 valuations face refinancing in a higher-for-longer rate environment.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Peak-vintage LBO refinancing and private-credit stress are the key downside vectors; monitor refinancing walls and software-portfolio marks.
insights.locator: The outlook, however, is not without risks.

insights.claim: Venture capital is concentrating increasingly around AI at a pace reminiscent of previous technology booms, with the central question for H2 2026 being whether exit markets will validate the confidence already reflected in deployment.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: The boom-like AI VC concentration raises bubble risk; the validation signal will come from exit markets, not further deployment.

themes.theme: Playbook for the AI-Shaped Deal Market

insights.claim: Acquirers should define an AI deal thesis before launching an acquisition, identifying needed AI capabilities, evaluating build-versus-buy tradeoffs at current valuations, and using flexible structures like stepwise stakes, JVs, or earn-outs to avoid paying peak prices for unproven economics.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: As application-layer valuations correct in H2 2026, the premium for a clear thesis and the penalty for lacking one are the highest of the AI deal cycle.

insights.claim: Historical evidence shows companies that make selective, well-prepared acquisitions during periods of uncertainty consistently outperform those that wait for perfect conditions.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Preparation and speed-from-decision-to-term-sheet are themselves competitive advantages; macro uncertainty argues for readiness, not inaction.
insights.quote: companies that make selective, well-prepared acquisitions during periods of uncertainty consistently outperform those that wait for perfect conditions to arise.

insights.claim: The most common source of M&A value destruction is not overpayment at signing but underperformance in post-deal value creation, making always-on integration capability the highest-return M&A investment most companies can make.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: For AI targets, talent retention and tech-stack alignment are the hard problems; dedicated PMI teams and day-one readiness protect returns more than price discipline alone.
insights.quote: The most common source of M&A value destruction is not overpayment at signing, but underperformance in postdeal value creation.

insights.claim: Not all AI-related acquisitions should be evaluated through a traditional synergy lens; some are about securing tactical control over scarce assets and require flexible structures like JVs, minority investments, or exchanges of computing capacity for equity.
insights.speaker: Jens Kengelbach
insights.so_what: Full ownership is often neither feasible nor desirable in AI deals; governance structure design becomes critical to value capture.
insights.locator: Adjust your approach to value creation.
